setting.note: The young graduate Arkady brings his friend Bazarov, a self-declared nihilist who believes in nothing but science and reason, home to his father's estate, and the clash between the generations plays out across the estates and towns of provincial Russia. Turgenev maps a real rural world: the Kirsanov estate of Maryino, the provincial town and its society, the estate where Bazarov meets the woman who defeats his creed, and the humble farm of his devoted parents.

rights.originalWork: Public domain. Ivan Turgenev (d. 1883), first published 1862 (Ottsy i deti). Mapped as a cited work: we store no text and reproduce no passages here. The places are facts and the notes are our own.
rights.textSource: None stored. This entry reproduces no text; the public-domain original is widely available.
rights.territoryNotes: Locations and original commentary only. The original text is in the public domain.
rights.attribution: Fathers and Sons, Ivan Turgenev (1862). Locations mapped by Read the Places.

palette.note: The earth-red of the Russian countryside and of Bazarov's harsh new creed, the nihilism that would tear up the old ground and plant only what reason allows. A cited book's colour is not a quotation, because we store no text to quote; it still has to be legible, and this clears WCAG AA on paper.
